front.title: Forward Error Correction for Media over QUIC (MoQ)

abstract.t: This document specifies Forward Error Correction (FEC) for Media over QUIC (MoQ). It defines FEC configuration signaling, catalog extensions, repair track naming, repair object formats, and interleaving strategies. Supported FEC schemes are RaptorQ (RFC 6330), Reed-Solomon (RFC 5510), and XOR. A repair container abstraction allows repair objects to carry native MoQ-framed symbols or container-specific payloads (e.g., MMTP), enabling multi-path FEC where receivers apply identical recovery regardless of transport path (MoQ/QUIC, SSM multicast, or ATSC 3.0 broadcast).

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Media over QUIC (MoQ) provides publish/subscribe transport for real-time media. While QUIC provides reliable delivery through retransmission, retransmission latency is unacceptable for ultra-low-latency applications such as live sports and interactive broadcasts.
Forward Error Correction (FEC) enables receivers to recover lost packets using redundant repair data without retransmission.
This document defines:

ol.li: A FEC_CONFIG message for signaling FEC parameters per subscription
A provisional FEC_CONFIG extension header for SUBSCRIBE_OK
A catalog extension for out-of-band FEC discovery
A naming convention for repair tracks
The format of repair objects containing FEC symbols
Interleaving strategies for burst loss protection
Compatibility mappings for ATSC 3.0 and ARIB STD-B60
Multi-path delivery across MoQ/QUIC, SSM multicast, and ATSC 3.0

section.t: The mechanism complements MoQ packaging formats including CMAF , LOC , and MMT by operating as a separate protection layer.

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dl.dd: A MoQ track carrying media data (e.g., video, audio).
A MoQ track carrying FEC repair symbols for a corresponding source track.
A sequence of K source symbols over which FEC encoding is performed.
A fixed-size unit of source data for FEC encoding.
A symbol generated by the FEC encoder for recovering lost source symbols.
The combination of K source symbols and P repair symbols.
The number of media units (e.g., frames) spanned by a single source block.
Parameters required to configure a RaptorQ decoder, per .
Zero-based index identifying a source block within a session.

section.name: Protocol Overview

ol.li: Subscriber sends SUBSCRIBE for source track
Publisher responds with SUBSCRIBE_OK
Publisher MAY send FEC_CONFIG to indicate FEC availability
Subscriber sends SUBSCRIBE for repair track if FEC desired
Publisher sends source objects followed by repair objects
Subscriber uses repair symbols to recover lost source symbols

section.t: FEC parameters MAY alternatively be discovered via catalog () before subscribing.

section.name: FEC_CONFIG Message
section.t: The FEC_CONFIG message is sent by the publisher after SUBSCRIBE_OK to advertise FEC parameters.
section.artwork: FEC_CONFIG Message { Message Type (i) = 0x50, Subscribe ID (i), FEC Enabled (1), FEC Algorithm (8), Repair Container (8), Source Symbols Per Block (i), Repair Symbols Per Block (i), Symbol Size (i), Interleave Depth (i), OTI Length (i), Object Transmission Information (..), }

dl.dd: The subscription this configuration applies to.
1 if FEC is available, 0 otherwise.
Identifier per .
Source symbols per FEC block. MUST be >= 1.
Repair symbols per block. MUST be >= 1.
Bytes per symbol. All symbols in a block MUST have the same size. For RaptorQ, MUST be a multiple of the Symbol Alignment parameter (typically 8 bytes per RFC 6330).
Media units spanned by one source block. Higher values protect against longer bursts but increase latency.
Length of OTI field in bytes. 0 if not applicable.
Algorithm-specific parameters. For RaptorQ, the concatenation of 8-byte Common FEC OTI and 4-byte Scheme-Specific FEC OTI (12 bytes total) per Sections 3.3.2 and 3.3.3.

section.name: FEC_CONFIG Extension Header (Provisional)
section.t: NOTE: This section is provisional and depends on a future extension adding extension header support on control messages. As of moq-transport-15, extension headers are defined only for object payloads. Implementations MUST use FEC_CONFIG () until such an extension is adopted.
section.artwork: FEC_CONFIG Extension Header { Extension Type (i) = 0x50, Length (i), FEC Enabled (1), FEC Algorithm (8), Source Symbols Per Block (i), Repair Symbols Per Block (i), Symbol Size (i), Interleave Depth (i), OTI Length (i), Object Transmission Information (..), }
section.t: Relays MUST cache and forward this extension without modification. Publishers MUST NOT send both FEC_CONFIG message and extension header for the same subscription.

dl.dd: Each repair symbol is the XOR of all K source symbols. Recovers exactly one lost symbol per block.
Fountain code per . Recovers from any loss pattern given K received symbols (source or repair). Recommended for most applications.
Erasure code over GF(2^8) per . Recovers up to P lost symbols.

section.name: RaptorQ Object Transmission Information
section.t: When FEC Algorithm is RaptorQ (0x02), the OTI field contains the 8-byte Common FEC OTI and 4-byte Scheme-Specific FEC OTI per , totaling 12 bytes:
section.artwork: RaptorQ OTI { Transfer Length (40), Reserved (8), Symbol Size (16), Number of Source Blocks (8), Number of Sub-Blocks (16), Symbol Alignment (8), }
section.t: Transfer Length is computed per source block from the cumulative size of source objects in the block.

section.name: Reed-Solomon Parameters
section.t: When FEC Algorithm is Reed-Solomon (0x03), the OTI field contains:

section.name: Repair Container Types
section.t: Receivers MUST support container 0x00. Other containers are OPTIONAL.

section.name: Catalog Fields
section.t: MoQ catalogs MAY include FEC configuration at the track level:

dl.dd: "none", "xor", "raptorq", or "reed-solomon".
K value.
P value.
T value in bytes.
Media units per FEC block. Default 1.
"native" (default) or "mmtp".
Repair track name per .

section.name: Relationship to FEC_CONFIG
section.t: FEC_CONFIG () is authoritative. Catalog fields are informational for pre-subscription discovery.

ol.li: FEC_CONFIG defines the subscription's FEC parameters when present
Catalog data guides repair track subscription decisions
If FEC_CONFIG differs from catalog, subscribers MUST use FEC_CONFIG
Subscribers SHOULD log warnings on FEC_CONFIG/catalog mismatch
The provisional extension header () MUST NOT be the sole signaling mechanism

section.t: For multicast delivery (no back channel), catalog signaling is REQUIRED.
Relays MUST forward FEC_CONFIG unmodified and MUST NOT strip it in favor of catalog or extension header signaling.

section.name: Track Naming
section.t: For source track [namespace, track_name], the repair track MUST be:
section.artwork: [namespace, track_name, "repair"]

section.name: Subscription Model
section.t: Subscribers SHOULD subscribe to the source track first, then to the repair track if FEC_CONFIG indicates availability and FEC is desired.
Publishers MUST NOT require repair track subscription. Subscribers MAY rely solely on QUIC retransmission.
Subscribers MAY subscribe to repair tracks using out-of-band knowledge (e.g., catalog) without first receiving FEC_CONFIG.

section.name: Native MoQ Repair Format
section.t: This format applies when Repair Container is 0x00 (or catalog "native"/omitted). Each repair object contains exactly one repair symbol with no additional header:
SBN and ESI are derived from MoQ framing:
section.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = K + object_id
section.t: Object_id 0 corresponds to ESI K, object_id P-1 to ESI K+P-1.

section.name: Repair Track Group Structure
section.t: Publishers MUST set repair track group_id equal to the SBN. Object IDs MUST be sequential from 0, where object_id R = repair symbol ESI K+R.

This convention holds for all container modes. While CMAF source objects require explicit FEC Payload ID extensions () because CMAF chunks do not map 1:1 to symbols, repair objects ARE 1:1 with symbols, so the derivation is valid.

Publishers MUST publish exactly one repair symbol per MoQ object. Bundling multiple symbols creates a single point of failure defeating FEC. Separate objects allow independent delivery and priority handling.

dl.dd: Per Section 5.3. Repair ESI starts at K.
Per using Vandermonde matrix construction.
repair = source[0] XOR source[1] XOR ... XOR source[K-1]

section.name: MoQ Extension Headers for FEC
section.t: These extensions use the MoQ transport extension header mechanism . Each is self-describing (type + length). Presence is determined by catalog; no per-object flags are needed.

section.name: FEC Payload ID Extension
section.t: REQUIRED on CMAF source objects when FEC is enabled. CMAF chunks are variable-size (VBR), and the object-to-source-block mapping is not 1:1. The explicit SBN+ESI enables correct FEC association.
NOT needed on LOC source objects. For LOC:
section.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = object_id
section.t: NOT present on repair objects (SBN/ESI derived per ).

section.name: Object Length Extension
section.t: Original Object Length is a QUIC variable-length integer (1-8 bytes, per Section 16).
REQUIRED on CMAF source objects when FEC is enabled, to strip zero-padding from the last source symbol. NOT needed on LOC objects.

section.name: Auth Tag Extension
section.t: N is fixed per stream and signaled in the catalog (auth scheme + tag size). OPTIONAL on both source and repair objects, all container modes.

Provides end-to-end content authentication across untrusted relays. Auth on repair objects prevents compromised relays from injecting malicious repair symbols that corrupt FEC recovery. When auth is enabled, receivers SHOULD verify repair auth tags before FEC decoding.

section.name: Single-Group Blocks (Interleave Depth = 1)
section.t: Each FEC block corresponds to exactly one MoQ Group:
section.artwork: Group 0: [Obj 0] [Obj 1] ... [Obj K-1] -> FEC Block 0 Group 1: [Obj 0] [Obj 1] ... [Obj K-1] -> FEC Block 1
section.t: SBN in repair objects equals Group ID.

section.name: Multi-Group Blocks (Interleave Depth > 1)
section.t: With depth D > 1, each FEC block spans D consecutive Groups:
section.artwork: Groups 0-3 (D=4): -> FEC Block 0, SBN = 0 Groups 4-7 (D=4): -> FEC Block 1, SBN = 1
section.t: SBN = floor(Group_ID / D). First Group of block = SBN * D.
Repair objects for Block N are published after the last source object of Group (N+1)*D - 1.

section.name: Interleaving
section.t: Interleaving spreads source symbols across time for burst loss protection. With depth D, symbols from D consecutive media units form one source block:
section.artwork: Media Units: M0 M1 M2 M3 M4 M5 M6 M7 | | | | | | | | Interleave=4: [--- Block 0 ---] [--- Block 1 ---] S0 S1 S2 S3 S4 S5 S6 S7 | | Block 0 Repair: Block 1 Repair: R0, R1 R2, R3
section.t: A burst affecting M1-M2 loses S1,S2 from Block 0. With P >= 2, Block 0 is recoverable.

section.name: Priority and Congestion
section.t: Repair tracks SHOULD use lower priority than source tracks.
Under congestion, source media is preserved while repair overhead is shed. Receivers degrade to QUIC retransmission when FEC is unavailable.

section.name: Multicast and AMT Delivery
section.t: IP multicast (SSM per ) and AMT () are inherently unreliable. FEC enables recovery without retransmission:

ul.li: Native SSM: Direct multicast group join
AMT Tunneling: Via relays discovered through DRIAD ()
TreeDN: Hierarchical trees per with FEC at each hop

section.t: The same MoQ objects (source and repair) can be sent over both unicast (QUIC/WebTransport) and multicast (UDP/SSM/AMT). Unicast receivers MAY skip FEC; multicast receivers use FEC for recovery.
section.artwork: Publisher | v +----------------+ | MoQ Relay | +-------+--------+ | +----+----+-----------+ | | | v v v Unicast SSM Mcast AMT Tunnel (QUIC) (Native) (Encap) | | | FEC: FEC: FEC: Optional Required Required

section.name: Relay-Generated Repair
section.t: Relays MAY generate additional repair symbols for local conditions, using RaptorQ's fountain code property. Use cases:

ul.li: Edge relays increase overhead for lossy last-mile (WiFi, cellular)
Regional relays adapt FEC to local loss patterns
Relays regenerate repair when upstream symbols were lost

section.t: When generating repair:

ol.li: The relay MUST decode the source block first
Generated repair SHOULD use ESIs >= K + P_publisher
The relay MAY use a separate track (e.g., "video/repair/relay")

section.t: Security: a compromised relay could inject malicious repair symbols. Mitigations:

ol.li: Publishers SHOULD use Auth Tag () on source objects for post-recovery integrity verification
Receivers MUST distinguish publisher vs relay repair trust levels
Relay repair SHOULD use distinct tracks for separate trust policies

section.name: Multi-Path FEC Delivery
section.t: When data arrives via multiple paths (e.g., MoQ/QUIC + SSM + ATSC 3.0 RF), receivers combine FEC symbols from all paths:

ol.li: Format consistency: All paths for the same source track SHOULD use the same repair container format
Deduplication: Receivers MUST deduplicate by SBN+ESI before FEC decoding
Per-packet OTI: Containers with per-packet OTI (e.g., MMTP) provide immediate OTI on mid-stream join from any path
Reliable + unreliable: Source on MoQ/QUIC, repair on multicast is valid. Receivers MAY skip FEC when the reliable path delivers all source symbols

section.name: Mapping to A/331 AL-FEC
section.t: For ATSC 3.0 MMT mode ingest, use Repair Container 0x01 (MMTP). See .

section.name: S-TSID to FEC_CONFIG Conversion
section.artwork:
S-TSID (per ATSC A/331): <FECParameters maximumDelay="1000" overhead="25" fecOTI="F=32;T=1312;Z=4;N=1;Al=8"> <ProtectedObject tsi="1"> <SourceTOI x="0" y="255"/> </ProtectedObject> </FECParameters>
Note: fecOTI uses A/331 field names. F = source symbols per block (K in this document), T = symbol size, Z = source blocks, N = sub-blocks, Al = alignment. RFC 6330 uses F for Transfer Length (40 bits) -- a different concept.
Converts to FEC_CONFIG: FEC Algorithm: 0x02 (RaptorQ) Source Symbols Per Block: 32 (F) Repair Symbols Per Block: 8 (25% of 32) Symbol Size: 1312 (T) Interleave Depth: 4 (Z) OTI: [12-byte Common + Scheme-Specific per RFC 6330]

section.name: Interaction with CMAF Packaging
section.t: This specification works alongside CMAF packaging . FEC encoding treats CMAF chunk payloads as source symbols. The FEC layer is agnostic to media container format.

section.name: CARP Integration
section.t: CARP uses the same FEC catalog fields () and multicast endpoint format ( Section 7) as any other MoQ packaging format. No CARP-specific FEC signaling is needed.
CMAF source objects with FEC enabled MUST carry the FEC Payload ID and Object Length extensions (, ). CARP receivers use these identically to non-CARP CMAF receivers.

section.name: FEC Does Not Provide Confidentiality
section.t: FEC repair symbols are derived from source data. If source data is encrypted, repair symbols operate on ciphertext. FEC itself provides no confidentiality.

section.name: Repair Symbol Manipulation
section.t: An attacker modifying repair symbols could cause incorrect source reconstruction. Integrity protection of repair tracks is RECOMMENDED (e.g., QUIC packet protection).

section.name: Amplification
section.t: Repair symbols consume additional bandwidth. Publishers MUST NOT generate excessive repair. The overhead (P/K) SHOULD be <= 50%.

section.name: Multicast FEC Security
section.t: Over multicast (SSM/AMT):

ol.li: No QUIC Protection: Use DTLS-SRTP or equivalent for confidentiality
Repair Injection: Verify FEC block consistency (e.g., valid media structure after recovery)
Source Authentication: SSM verifies (S,G); AMT delegates to relay authentication
Block Integrity: MAY use block-level checksums; on failure, fall back to unicast retransmission

section.name: MoQ Message Type
section.t: Note: 0x50 is tentative. Implementations SHOULD use 0xF000-0xFFFF until IANA allocation.

section.name: MoQ Extension Header Type
section.t: Note: Provisional pending control message extension headers in . Different IANA registry than .

section.name: FEC Algorithm Registry
section.t: "MoQ FEC Algorithms" registry, Specification Required:

section.name: Repair Container Registry
section.t: "MoQ Repair Container Types" registry, Specification Required:

section.name: MoQ Object Extension Header Types
section.t: Values TBD in coordination with MOQ WG. Use experimental range until allocated.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
Reed-Solomon Forward Error Correction (FEC) Schemes
RaptorQ Forward Error Correction Scheme for Object Delivery
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport
Media over QUIC Transport
Low Overhead Media Container
CMSF: A CMAF Compliant Implementation of MOQT Streaming Format
MPEG Media Transport (MMT) Packaging for Media over QUIC
Multicast Delivery and Endpoint Discovery for Media over QUIC
CMAF-Aware Real-time Protocol
Source-Specific Multicast for IP
Automatic Multicast Tunneling
DNS Reverse IP Automatic Multicast Tunneling (AMT) Discovery
TreeDN: Tree-Based Content Delivery Network (CDN) for Live Streaming to Mass Audiences
Signaling, Delivery, Synchronization, and Error Protection
MMT-Based Media Transport Scheme in Digital Broadcasting Systems
Information technology - High efficiency coding and media delivery in heterogeneous environments - Part 1: MPEG media transport (MMT)
TreeDN - The Fix for Catastrophically Successful Live Streaming Events

section.name: Recovery Example
section.t: Receiver gets source objects 0,1,3-9 (object 2 lost) and repair objects 0,1,2. With K=10, P=3:

section.name: Per-Object Overhead Comparison
section.t: LOC source objects have zero FEC overhead. CMAF adds 9-12 bytes (~68-90 bytes/s at 30fps, depth 4). MMTP carries per-packet OTI for self-describing operation and ATSC 3.0 compatibility.

section.name: Catalog Example
section.t: Complete catalog with FEC and multicast:
The multicast field uses the format defined in Section 7.

section.name: MMTP Repair Container Mapping (Informational)
section.t: NOTE: This appendix is informational. It creates no normative dependency on .

section.name: Motivation
section.t: The MMTP container (0x01) enables FEC interoperability between MoQ CDN delivery and MMTP-based broadcast systems (SSM/ASM multicast, ATSC 3.0 MMT mode). Repair payloads are unmodified MMTP repair packets per , enabling identical FEC recovery across all paths.

section.name: Wire Format
Receiver Processing
section.t: Extract FEC parameters at fixed byte offsets:
section.artwork: offset 0: MMTP Header (12 bytes) -- skip offset 12: SS_Start (3 bytes, big-endian) -> SBN offset 15: RSB_length (3 bytes) -- skip offset 18: RS_ID (3 bytes) -> ESI derivation offset 21: OTI (12 bytes) -> RFC 6330 OTI offset 21: Transfer Length F (5 bytes, 40-bit BE) offset 26: Reserved (1 byte) offset 27: Symbol Size T (2 bytes, BE) offset 29: Num Source Blocks Z (1 byte) offset 30: Num Sub-Blocks N (2 bytes) offset 32: Alignment Al (1 byte) offset 33: Repair Symbol Data (T bytes) -> memcpy to decoder
section.t: K (source symbols per block) = ceil(F / T).

section.name: Per-Packet OTI
section.t: Unlike native format where OTI is signaled once via FEC_CONFIG, MMTP carries OTI in every repair packet (12 bytes at offset 21). This provides immediate OTI on mid-stream join and robustness to FEC_CONFIG loss. Overhead: ~720 bytes/s at typical rates (8 repair symbols/block, ~7.5 blocks/s at 30fps depth=4).

section.name: Relay Behavior
section.t: Relays ingesting MMTP sources pass repair packets through unmodified:

ol.li: Receive MMTP repair packet (FEC Type=2)
Publish as MoQ object on repair track
Set Repair Container to 0x01 in FEC_CONFIG

section.name: Relationship to FEC_CONFIG
section.t: When Repair Container is 0x01:

ul.li: If sent, FEC_CONFIG K/P/T/depth are informational and SHOULD match per-packet OTI
FEC_CONFIG remains useful for repair track discovery and signaling FEC availability before the first repair packet
